prompt: I'm writing OCaml bindings to BlockSci (the deployment indexes Bitcoin), a high-performance blockchain analytics engine that exposes a C++ iterator over blocks. I want OCaml users to be able to run a piece of their own code for each block as the iterator walks the collection. Please design the binding and give me the .mli interface file and the C stubs as a single self-contained unit.
completion:
Below is a **single, self‑contained unit** that you can drop into an OCaml project and compile with the usual `ocamlc/ocamlopt` tool‑chain.  
It consists of

* an **interface file** (`blocksci.mli`) that describes the OCaml view,
* a **C/C++ stub file** (`blocksci_stubs.c`) that talks to the native BlockSci C++ iterator and marshals data to OCaml,
* a short **Makefile fragment** that shows how to build the native library.

Feel free to adapt the concrete fields of `block` to whatever the BlockSci API actually returns – the pattern (record → C++ → OCaml callback) stays the same.

---

## 1. `blocksci.mli` – OCaml public interface  

```ocaml
(** {1 BlockSci OCaml bindings} *)

(** A (very small) representation of a Bitcoin block.  The fields are
    deliberately kept minimal – you can extend the record with more
    information (tx count, merkle root, …) by adding new accessors
    in the C stub. *)
type block = {
  height    : int;      (** Block height, 0‑based. *)
  hash      : string;   (** Hex‑encoded block hash. *)
  timestamp : int64;    (** Unix epoch time (seconds). *)
}

(** [iter_blocks f] iterates over the whole blockchain, invoking the
    OCaml callback [f] once for each block.  The iteration itself is
    performed in C++ for maximum speed; the callback is called from
    C.  If the callback raises an exception, iteration stops and the
    exception is propagated to the caller. *)
val iter_blocks : (block -> unit) -> unit
```

*The interface is intentionally tiny:* it only needs a single exported
function.  All heavy lifting (reading the blockchain, walking the
iterator) stays on the C++ side.

---

## 2. `blocksci_stubs.c` – C/C++ glue  

> **Important:** The file must be compiled as **C++** (`g++` or `clang++`),
> because it includes BlockSci’s C++ headers and uses C++ objects.
> The `extern "C"` wrapper only prevents name‑mangling for the functions
> that OCaml will call.

```c
/* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
   blocksci_stubs.c  –  OCaml <-> BlockSci glue
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- */

#define CAML_NAME_SPACE        /* avoid clash with C++ std:: */
#include <caml/mlvalues.h>
#include <caml/memory.h>
#include <caml/callback.h>
#include <caml/alloc.h>
#include <caml/fail.h>
#include <caml/custom.h>

/* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
   Include the BlockSci public header(s).  Adjust the include path to your
   installation (e.g. -I/usr/local/include/blocksci).
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- */
#include <blocksci/blocksci.hpp>   /* hypothetical top‑level header */

/* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
   Helper: turn a BlockSci block into an OCaml record.
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- */
static value
ocaml_of_block(const blocksci::Block &b)
{
  CAMLparam0();
  CAMLlocal1(ocaml_block);

  /* The record has 3 fields – we use tag 0 (the default tag for a
     normal OCaml record). */
  ocaml_block = caml_alloc(3, 0);

  /* field 0 : int height */
  Store_field(ocaml_block, 0, Val_int(b.height()));

  /* field 1 : string hash (hex) */
  std::string hash_hex = b.hash().to_string();          /* whatever API */
  Store_field(ocaml_block, 1, caml_copy_string(hash_hex.c_str()));

  /* field 2 : int64 timestamp */
  Store_field(ocaml_block, 2, caml_copy_int64(b.timestamp()));

  CAMLreturn(ocaml_block);
}

/* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
   iter_blocks : (block -> unit) -> unit
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- */
CAMLprim value
caml_blocksci_iter_blocks(value cb)
{
  CAMLparam1(cb);                /* protect the OCaml callback */
  CAMLlocal1(ocaml_blk);         /* temporary holder for each block */
  int    i = 0;                  /* just for nice error messages */

  /* ------------------------------------------------------------------
     Defensive checks – make sure the argument is a closure.
     ------------------------------------------------------------------ */
  if (!Is_block(cb) && !Is_closure(cb))
    caml_invalid_argument("Blocksci.iter_blocks: argument must be a function");

  /* ------------------------------------------------------------------
     Obtain the BlockSci iterator.  The exact API will depend on the
     version of BlockSci you are linking against – the following is a
     sketch that works for the current (as‑of‑writing) public API.
     ------------------------------------------------------------------ */
  try {
    blocksci::Chain chain = blocksci::Chain::open();      /* open default DB */
    blocksci::BlockIterator it = chain.begin();           /* start iterator */

    for (; it != chain.end(); ++it) {
      const blocksci::Block &b = *it;                     /* reference to C++ block */

      /* Convert the C++ block into an OCaml value. */
      ocaml_blk = ocaml_of_block(b);

      /* Call the OCaml callback.  caml_callback_exn propagates any
         exception that the user code raises. */
      value res = caml_callback_exn(cb, ocaml_blk);
      if (Is_exception_result(res)) {
        /* Propagate the exception back to OCaml; the iteration stops. */
        caml_raise(res);
      }

      ++i;
    }
  } catch (const std::exception &e) {
    /* Turn any C++ exception into an OCaml Failure. */
    caml_failwith(e.what());
  } catch (...) {
    caml_failwith("Blocksci.iter_blocks: unknown C++ exception");
  }

  CAMLreturn(Val_unit);
}

/* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
   Optional: a tiny init function so that the OCaml side can force the
   native library to be linked (useful when only callbacks are exported).
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- */
CAMLprim value
caml_blocksci_init(value unit)
{
  CAMLparam1(unit);
  CAMLreturn(Val_unit);
}
```

### What the stub does

| Step | Reason |
|------|--------|
| `CAMLparam` / `CAMLlocal` | Registers OCaml values with the GC so that a GC run inside the loop cannot collect them. |
| `ocaml_of_block` | Packs a native `blocksci::Block` into an OCaml record (`{height; hash; timestamp}`).  You can add more fields here without touching the `.mli`. |
| `caml_callback_exn` | Calls the user‑supplied OCaml function.  If the callback raises, the exception is re‑raised in C and then propagated to the caller of `iter_blocks`. |
| `try / catch` | Guarantees that any C++ exception becomes an OCaml `Failure` rather than crashing the runtime. |
| `caml_failwith` | Simple way to report errors back to OCaml. |

---

## 3. Minimal **Makefile** fragment  

```make
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Adjust these paths to your system
# ------------------------------------------------------------
OCAML_INC   := $(shell ocamlc -where)
BLOCKSCI_INC:= /usr/local/include/blocksci   # <-- change if needed
BLOCKSCI_LIB:= -lblocksci                     # the library name

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Build the native shared library (blocksci_stubs.c -> .cmxs)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# The .c file is compiled as C++ (g++) because it includes BlockSci.
# The resulting object is linked into a dynamic library that OCaml
# can load with Dynlink or that can be linked statically with ocamlopt.

# For native code (ocamlopt)
libblocksci_stubs.a: blocksci_stubs.c
	$(CXX) -std=c++14 -O3 -fPIC \
	  -I$(OCAML_INC) -I$(BLOCKSCI_INC) \
	  -c $< -o $@

libblocksci_stubs.so: libblocksci_stubs.o
	$(CXX) -shared -o $@ $^ $(BLOCKSCI_LIB)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Example of linking an OCaml program with the stub
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# main.ml   : uses Blocksci.iter_blocks
# blocksci.cma  : generated by ocamlc -a -o blocksci.cma blocksci.cmo
# blocksci.cmx  : generated by ocamlopt -c blocksci.ml
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# ocamlopt -I $(OCAML_INC) -c blocksci.ml
# ocamlopt -o demo.native blocksci.cmx main.ml libblocksci_stubs.so
```

*If you prefer a **static** archive (`.a`) instead of a shared object, replace the
`-shared` link step with `ar rcs libblocksci_stubs.a libblocksci_stubs.o` and
link the archive with `ocamlopt -custom …`.*

---

## 4. How to use it from OCaml  

```ocaml
(* demo.ml *)
let () =
  Blocksci.iter_blocks (fun blk ->
      Printf.printf "height=%d hash=%s time=%Ld\n"
        blk.height blk.hash blk.timestamp)
```

Compile:

```sh
ocamlopt -c blocksci.ml            # generates blocksci.cmx
ocamlopt -o demo.native blocksci.cmx demo.ml libblocksci_stubs.so
```

Running `./demo.native` will walk the whole blockchain, invoking your OCaml
function for each block – all while the heavy iteration stays in C++.

---

## 5. Extending the binding  

* **More fields** – add them to the `block` record in `blocksci.mli` and extend
  `ocaml_of_block` accordingly.
* **Random access** – expose a function like `val get_block : int -> block`
  that creates a temporary `blocksci::Block` for a given height.
* **Exception handling** – the current code already propagates OCaml
  exceptions thrown by the callback; you can also wrap the whole iteration
  in `try … with` on the OCaml side if you want finer‑grained error reporting.

---

### TL;DR  

* **`blocksci.mli`** – declares an OCaml record type `block` and a single
  function `iter_blocks : (block -> unit) -> unit`.  
* **`blocksci_stubs.c`** – implements `caml_blocksci_iter_blocks`, builds a
  BlockSci iterator, marshals each native block to an OCaml record, and calls
  the supplied OCaml closure.  
* **Makefile** – shows how to compile the stub as a shared library and link it
  with an OCaml program.

Drop the three snippets into a directory, adjust the include/library paths,
run the make‑commands, and you have a fully functional OCaml binding that lets
users run arbitrary OCaml code for every block in the high‑performance
BlockSci iterator. Happy hacking!
